<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:أوتوغيزوشي]][[de:Otogizōshi]][[en:Otogi-zōshi]][[fa:اوتوگی زوشی]][[fr:Otogi-zōshi]][[id:Otogizōshi]][[it:Otogizōshi]][[ja:御伽草子]][[jv:Otogizōshi]][[ko:오토기조시]][[pl:Otogizōshi]][[ru:Отоги-дзоси]][[simple:Otogizōshi]][[tr:Otogizōshi]][[uk:Отоґі-дзосі]][[zh:御伽草子]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q82440]]
Wikidata name: otogizōshi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Japanese literary genre}}
{{about|medieval Japanese illustrated short stories|the anime series|Otogi Zoshi (anime)|the short stories by Osamu Dazai|Otogi-zōshi (Dazai)}}
{{one source|date=January 2023}}
{{italic title}}
[[Image:Tale of Issun Bōshi from Otogi-zōshi.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3|right|Illustration from {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} tale, published {{c.|1725}}]]

{{nihongo||御伽草子|'''Otogi-zōshi'''}} are a group of about 350 Japanese prose narratives written primarily in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (1392–1573).  These illustrated short stories, which remain unattributed, together form one of the representative literary genres of the Japanese medieval era.

== Overview ==
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} is a general term for narrative literature written between the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (approximately 1336–1573) and the beginning of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603–1867).{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} The term originates with a mid-Edo collection of 23 stories, titled {{nihongo||御伽文庫|Otogi Bunko}} or {{nihongo||御伽草紙/御伽草子|Otogi-zōshi}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} It later came to denote other works of the same genre and period.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Modern scholarship sometimes distinguishes between "true" {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}, covering only the 23 works included in the aforementioned collection, and other works that it instead terms {{nihongo||室町時代物語|Muromachi-jidai monogatari}} or {{nihongo||中世小説|chūsei shōsetsu}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} 

===List===
The 23 tales covered by the narrow definition are:
*{{nihongo||文正草子|[[Bunshō-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||鉢かづき|[[Hachikazuki]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||小町草紙|[[:en:Komachi Sōshi|Komachi Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||御曹子島渡り|[[Onzōshi Shima-watari]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||唐糸草紙|[[Karaito-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||木幡狐|[[Kowata-gitsune]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||七草草紙|[[Nanakusa Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||猿源氏草紙|[[Saru Genji Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||物くさ太郎|[[Monokusa Tarō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||さざれ石|[[Sazare-ishi (otogi-zōshi)|Sazare-ishi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||蛤の草紙|[[Hamaguri no Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||小敦盛|[[Ko-Atsumori]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||二十四孝|[[:en:Nijūshi-kō|Nijūshi-kō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||梵天国|[[Bongengoku]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||のせ猿草子|[[Nose-zaru Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||猫の草紙|[[Neko no Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||浜出|[[Hamade (otogi-zōshi)|Hamade]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||和泉式部|[[Izumi Shikibu (otogi-zōshi)|Izumi Shikibu]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||一寸法師|[[Issun-bōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||さいき|[[Saiki (otogi-zōshi)|Saiki]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||浦嶋太郎|[[:en:Urashima Tarō (otogi-zōshi)|Urashima Tarō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||横笛草紙|[[Yokobue-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||酒呑童子|[[:en:Shuten-dōji|Shutendōji]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

Under the broad definition, there are around 500 surviving examples of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Most are around 30–40 pages in length,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and are of uncertain date.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Their authors are also largely unknown,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} but whereas [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] and [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura]] {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Monogatari|Monogatari]]}} were almost all composed by members of the aristocracy, these works were composed by not just aristocrats but also [[:en:Buddhist monk|Buddhist monk]]s, hermits, educated members of the [[:en:Samurai|warrior class]].{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Some of the later {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} may have been written by members of the emerging urban merchant class.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Similarly, the works' intended readership was probably broader than the {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}} of earlier eras.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} They therefore have a wide variety of contents and draw material from various literary works of the past.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Based on their contents, scholars have divided them into six genres:{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||公家物|kuge-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||宗教物|shūkyō-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||武家物|buke-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||庶民物|shomin-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||外国物|gaikoku-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||異類物|irui-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

==={{transliteration|ja|Kuge-mono}}===
{{transliteration|ja|Kuge-mono}} are tales of the aristocracy.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} They mark a continuation of the earlier {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}} literature,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and are noted for the influence of ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Many of them are rewritten or abridged versions of earlier works.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Among the romantic works in this sub-genre are {{nihongo||忍音物語|[[Shinobine Monogatari]]}} and {{nihongo||若草物語|[[Wakakusa Monogatari]]}},{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and most end sadly with the characters cutting themselves off from society ({{nihongo||悲恋遁世|hiren tonsei}}).{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

==Categories==
[[Image:Otogizoshi4.jpg|thumb|upright=1.5|left|Cover from volume of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} tales, published {{c.|1725}}]]
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} have been broken down into multiple categories: tales of the [[:en:Aristocracy|Aristocracy]], which are derived from earlier works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''; religious tales; tales of warriors, often based on ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', the {{transliteration|ja|[[Taiheiki]]}}, ''[[:en:The Tale of the Soga|The Tale of the Soga]]'' and the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Gikeiki|Gikeiki]]}} (''The Tale of Yoshitsune''); tales of foreign countries, based on the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatarishū]]}}. The most well-known of the tales, however, are retellings of familiar [[Japanese mythology|legend]]s and [[Japanese folklore|folktale]]s, such as {{transliteration|ja|[[Issun-bōshi]]}}, the story of a one-inch-tall boy who overcomes countless obstacles to achieve success in the capital.

== Origins of the term {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} ==
The term {{transliteration|ja|otogi}} literally means '', with the full name of the genre translating to 'companion tale'. This designation, however, did not come into use until 1725, when a publisher in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]] released a set of 23 illustrated booklets titled {{transliteration|ja|[[Shūgen otogibunko]]}} (''Fortuitous Companion Library''). As other publishers produced their own versions of {{transliteration|ja|Shūgen otogibunko}}, they began referring to the set of tales as {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}. Gradually the term came to describe any work from the Muromachi or early Edo period that exhibited the same general style as the tales in {{transliteration|ja|Shūgen otogibunko}}.

== History of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} scholarship ==
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} came to the attention of modern [[:en:Literary criticism|literary historian]]s in the late 19th century. For the most part, scholars have been critical of this genre, dismissing it for its perceived faults when compared to the aristocratic literature of the Heian and Kamakura periods. As a result, standardized [[:en:Education in Japan|Japanese school]] [[:en:Textbook|Textbook]]s often omit any reference to {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} from their discussions of [[:en:medieval Japanese literature|medieval Japanese literature]]. Recent studies, however, have contradicted this critical stance, highlighting the vitality and inherent appeal of this underappreciated genre. The term {{transliteration|ja|chusei shosetsu}} ('medieval novels'), coined by eminent scholar Ichiko Teiji, attempts to situate the tales within a narrative continuum.

== List of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} ==
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Aisome-gawa (otogi-zōshi)|Aisome-gawa]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Aki no Yo no Naga Monogatari|Aki no Yo no Naga Monogatari]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ame no Wakahiko sōshi (otogi-zōshi)|Ame no Wakahiko sōshi]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Komachi Sōshi|Komachi Sōshi]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Koshikibu (one-volume otogi-zōshi)|Koshikibu]]}} (I)
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Koshikibu (two-volume otogi-zōshi)|Koshikibu]]}} (II)
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Urashima Tarō (otogi-zōshi)|Urashima Tarō (otogi-zōshi)]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Utatane no Sōshi|Utatane no Sōshi]]}}

Wikidata name: otogizōshi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Japanese literary genre}}
{{about|medieval Japanese illustrated short stories|the anime series|Otogi Zoshi (anime)|the short stories by Osamu Dazai|Otogi-zōshi (Dazai)}}
{{one source|date=January 2023}}
{{italic title}}
[[Image:Tale of Issun Bōshi from Otogi-zōshi.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3|right|Illustration from {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} tale, published {{c.|1725}}]]

{{nihongo||御伽草子|'''Otogi-zōshi'''}} are a group of about 350 Japanese prose narratives written primarily in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (1392–1573).  These illustrated short stories, which remain unattributed, together form one of the representative literary genres of the Japanese medieval era.

== Overview ==
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} is a general term for narrative literature written between the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (approximately 1336–1573) and the beginning of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603–1867).{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} The term originates with a mid-Edo collection of 23 stories, titled {{nihongo||御伽文庫|Otogi Bunko}} or {{nihongo||御伽草紙/御伽草子|Otogi-zōshi}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} It later came to denote other works of the same genre and period.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Modern scholarship sometimes distinguishes between "true" {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}, covering only the 23 works included in the aforementioned collection, and other works that it instead terms {{nihongo||室町時代物語|Muromachi-jidai monogatari}} or {{nihongo||中世小説|chūsei shōsetsu}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} 

===List===
The 23 tales covered by the narrow definition are:
*{{nihongo||文正草子|[[Bunshō-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||鉢かづき|[[Hachikazuki]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||小町草紙|[[:en:Komachi Sōshi|Komachi Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||御曹子島渡り|[[Onzōshi Shima-watari]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||唐糸草紙|[[Karaito-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||木幡狐|[[Kowata-gitsune]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||七草草紙|[[Nanakusa Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||猿源氏草紙|[[Saru Genji Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||物くさ太郎|[[Monokusa Tarō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||さざれ石|[[Sazare-ishi (otogi-zōshi)|Sazare-ishi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||蛤の草紙|[[Hamaguri no Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||小敦盛|[[Ko-Atsumori]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||二十四孝|[[:en:Nijūshi-kō|Nijūshi-kō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||梵天国|[[Bongengoku]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||のせ猿草子|[[Nose-zaru Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||猫の草紙|[[Neko no Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||浜出|[[Hamade (otogi-zōshi)|Hamade]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||和泉式部|[[Izumi Shikibu (otogi-zōshi)|Izumi Shikibu]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||一寸法師|[[Issun-bōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||さいき|[[Saiki (otogi-zōshi)|Saiki]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||浦嶋太郎|[[:en:Urashima Tarō (otogi-zōshi)|Urashima Tarō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||横笛草紙|[[Yokobue-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||酒呑童子|[[:en:Shuten-dōji|Shutendōji]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

Under the broad definition, there are around 500 surviving examples of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Most are around 30–40 pages in length,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and are of uncertain date.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Their authors are also largely unknown,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} but whereas [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] and [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura]] {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Monogatari|Monogatari]]}} were almost all composed by members of the aristocracy, these works were composed by not just aristocrats but also [[:en:Buddhist monk|Buddhist monk]]s, hermits, educated members of the [[:en:Samurai|warrior class]].{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Some of the later {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} may have been written by members of the emerging urban merchant class.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Similarly, the works' intended readership was probably broader than the {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}} of earlier eras.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} They therefore have a wide variety of contents and draw material from various literary works of the past.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Based on their contents, scholars have divided them into six genres:{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||公家物|kuge-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||宗教物|shūkyō-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||武家物|buke-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||庶民物|shomin-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||外国物|gaikoku-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||異類物|irui-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

==={{transliteration|ja|Kuge-mono}}===
{{transliteration|ja|Kuge-mono}} are tales of the aristocracy.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} They mark a continuation of the earlier {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}} literature,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and are noted for the influence of ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Many of them are rewritten or abridged versions of earlier works.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Among the romantic works in this sub-genre are {{nihongo||忍音物語|[[Shinobine Monogatari]]}} and {{nihongo||若草物語|[[Wakakusa Monogatari]]}},{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and most end sadly with the characters cutting themselves off from society ({{nihongo||悲恋遁世|hiren tonsei}}).{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

==Categories==
[[Image:Otogizoshi4.jpg|thumb|upright=1.5|left|Cover from volume of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} tales, published {{c.|1725}}]]
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} have been broken down into multiple categories: tales of the [[:en:Aristocracy|Aristocracy]], which are derived from earlier works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''; religious tales; tales of warriors, often based on ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', the {{transliteration|ja|[[Taiheiki]]}}, ''[[:en:The Tale of the Soga|The Tale of the Soga]]'' and the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Gikeiki|Gikeiki]]}} (''The Tale of Yoshitsune''); tales of foreign countries, based on the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatarishū]]}}. The most well-known of the tales, however, are retellings of familiar [[Japanese mythology|legend]]s and [[Japanese folklore|folktale]]s, such as {{transliteration|ja|[[Issun-bōshi]]}}, the story of a one-inch-tall boy who overcomes countless obstacles to achieve success in the capital.

== Origins of the term {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} ==
The term {{transliteration|ja|otogi}} literally means '', with the full name of the genre translating to 'companion tale'. This designation, however, did not come into use until 1725, when a publisher in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]] released a set of 23 illustrated booklets titled {{transliteration|ja|[[Shūgen otogibunko]]}} (''Fortuitous Companion Library''). As other publishers produced their own versions of {{transliteration|ja|Shūgen otogibunko}}, they began referring to the set of tales as {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}. Gradually the term came to describe any work from the Muromachi or early Edo period that exhibited the same general style as the tales in {{transliteration|ja|Shūgen otogibunko}}.

== History of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} scholarship ==
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} came to the attention of modern [[:en:Literary criticism|literary historian]]s in the late 19th century. For the most part, scholars have been critical of this genre, dismissing it for its perceived faults when compared to the aristocratic literature of the Heian and Kamakura periods. As a result, standardized [[:en:Education in Japan|Japanese school]] [[:en:Textbook|Textbook]]s often omit any reference to {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} from their discussions of [[:en:medieval Japanese literature|medieval Japanese literature]]. Recent studies, however, have contradicted this critical stance, highlighting the vitality and inherent appeal of this underappreciated genre. The term {{transliteration|ja|chusei shosetsu}} ('medieval novels'), coined by eminent scholar Ichiko Teiji, attempts to situate the tales within a narrative continuum.

== List of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} ==
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Aisome-gawa (otogi-zōshi)|Aisome-gawa]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Aki no Yo no Naga Monogatari|Aki no Yo no Naga Monogatari]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ame no Wakahiko sōshi (otogi-zōshi)|Ame no Wakahiko sōshi]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Komachi Sōshi|Komachi Sōshi]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Koshikibu (one-volume otogi-zōshi)|Koshikibu]]}} (I)
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Koshikibu (two-volume otogi-zōshi)|Koshikibu]]}} (II)
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Urashima Tarō (otogi-zōshi)|Urashima Tarō (otogi-zōshi)]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Utatane no Sōshi|Utatane no Sōshi]]}}

ORIGINAL, UNSOURCED DESCRIPTION (may be accurate, and wording may be better paraphrase of sourced version, so leaving intact but commented out for now):

This type of short prose narrative from [[Japan]] is often considered a transitional genre, which bridges the gap between the courtly literature of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794-1160) and the more [[Plebs|plebeian]] ''[[kanazōshi]]'' (tales written in [[kana]]) and ''[[ukiyo-zōshi]]'' (tales of the floating world) of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1600–1868).  The stories were originally recorded in both illustrated scrolls or booklets ([[Nara-ehon]]) which link the picture scrolls of the Heian era with the [[woodblock print]] books of the Edo period.  This suggests that ''otogi-zōshi'' were meant to be read aloud, with the illustrations serving as reference guides.

Because the stories were frequently recited before an audience, many narratives are simple, with little description, depth, or development. Others, however, are more poetically allusive, suggesting composition by literati. Many of the tales are straightforward [[quest]] narratives, often revolving around a single heroic figure.  In order to advance the plot, the passage of time is swift, with characters sometimes aging years or decades in the space of a single sentence.

The vast topical range of ''otogi-zōshi'' discourages easy generalization.  The subject matter includes worldly concerns (love, marriage, family); spiritual matters (the pursuit of enlightenment, encounters with manifestations of the Buddha); martial adventures; farce, and supernatural fantasy.  While some of the stories exhibit a clear didactic agenda, most ''otogi-zōshi'' appear to have been composed primarily for the sake of entertainment.
--><!-- https://ja.Wikipedia.org/wiki/市古貞次 -->

*{{nihongo||猿源氏草紙|[[Saru Genji Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

*{{nihongo||さざれ石|[[Sazare-ishi (otogi-zōshi)|Sazare-ishi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||蛤の草紙|[[Hamaguri no Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||小敦盛|[[Ko-Atsumori]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||二十四孝|[[:en:Nijūshi-kō|Nijūshi-kō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||梵天国|[[Bongengoku]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||のせ猿草子|[[Nose-zaru Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||猫の草紙|[[Neko no Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||浜出|[[Hamade (otogi-zōshi)|Hamade]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||和泉式部|[[Izumi Shikibu (otogi-zōshi)|Izumi Shikibu]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||一寸法師|[[Issun-bōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||さいき|[[Saiki (otogi-zōshi)|Saiki]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||浦嶋太郎|[[:en:Urashima Tarō (otogi-zōshi)|Urashima Tarō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||横笛草紙|[[Yokobue-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||酒呑童子|[[:en:Shuten-dōji|Shutendōji]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

Under the broad definition, there are around 500 surviving examples of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Most are around 30–40 pages in length,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and are of uncertain date.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Their authors are also largely unknown,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} but whereas [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] and [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura]] {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Monogatari|Monogatari]]}} were almost all composed by members of the aristocracy, these works were composed by not just aristocrats but also [[:en:Buddhist monk|Buddhist monk]]s, hermits, educated members of the [[:en:Samurai|warrior class]].{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Some of the later {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} may have been written by members of the emerging urban merchant class.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Similarly, the works' intended readership was probably broader than the {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}} of earlier eras.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} They therefore have a wide variety of contents and draw material from various literary works of the past.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Based on their contents, scholars have divided them into six genres:{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||公家物|kuge-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||宗教物|shūkyō-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||武家物|buke-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||庶民物|shomin-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||外国物|gaikoku-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||異類物|irui-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

==={{transliteration|ja|Kuge-mono}}===
{{transliteration|ja|Kuge-mono}} are tales of the aristocracy.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} They mark a continuation of the earlier {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}} literature,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and are noted for the influence of ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Many of them are rewritten or abridged versions of earlier works.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Among the romantic works in this sub-genre are {{nihongo||忍音物語|[[Shinobine Monogatari]]}} and {{nihongo||若草物語|[[Wakakusa Monogatari]]}},{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and most end sadly with the characters cutting themselves off from society ({{nihongo||悲恋遁世|hiren tonsei}}).{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

==Categories==
[[Image:Otogizoshi4.jpg|thumb|upright=1.5|left|Cover from volume of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} tales, published {{c.|1725}}]]
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} have been broken down into multiple categories: tales of the [[:en:Aristocracy|Aristocracy]], which are derived from earlier works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''; religious tales; tales of warriors, often based on ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', the {{transliteration|ja|[[Taiheiki]]}}, ''[[:en:The Tale of the Soga|The Tale of the Soga]]'' and the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Gikeiki|Gikeiki]]}} (''The Tale of Yoshitsune''); tales of foreign countries, based on the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatarishū]]}}. The most well-known of the tales, however, are retellings of familiar [[Japanese mythology|legend]]s and [[Japanese folklore|folktale]]s, such as {{transliteration|ja|[[Issun-bōshi]]}}, the story of a one-inch-tall boy who overcomes countless obstacles to achieve success in the capital.

== Origins of the term {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} ==
The term {{transliteration|ja|otogi}} literally means '', with the full name of the genre translating to 'companion tale'. This designation, however, did not come into use until 1725, when a publisher in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]] released a set of 23 illustrated booklets titled {{transliteration|ja|[[Shūgen otogibunko]]}} (''Fortuitous Companion Library''). As other publishers produced their own versions of {{transliteration|ja|Shūgen otogibunko}}, they began referring to the set of tales as {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}. Gradually the term came to describe any work from the Muromachi or early Edo period that exhibited the same general style as the tales in {{transliteration|ja|Shūgen otogibunko}}.

== History of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} scholarship ==
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} came to the attention of modern [[:en:Literary criticism|literary historian]]s in the late 19th century. For the most part, scholars have been critical of this genre, dismissing it for its perceived faults when compared to the aristocratic literature of the Heian and Kamakura periods. As a result, standardized [[:en:Education in Japan|Japanese school]] [[:en:Textbook|Textbook]]s often omit any reference to {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} from their discussions of [[:en:medieval Japanese literature|medieval Japanese literature]]. Recent studies, however, have contradicted this critical stance, highlighting the vitality and inherent appeal of this underappreciated genre. The term {{transliteration|ja|chusei shosetsu}} ('medieval novels'), coined by eminent scholar Ichiko Teiji, attempts to situate the tales within a narrative continuum.

ORIGINAL, UNSOURCED DESCRIPTION (may be accurate, and wording may be better paraphrase of sourced version, so leaving intact but commented out for now):

This type of short prose narrative from [[Japan]] is often considered a transitional genre, which bridges the gap between the courtly literature of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794-1160) and the more [[Plebs|plebeian]] ''[[kanazōshi]]'' (tales written in [[kana]]) and ''[[ukiyo-zōshi]]'' (tales of the floating world) of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1600–1868).  The stories were originally recorded in both illustrated scrolls or booklets ([[Nara-ehon]]) which link the picture scrolls of the Heian era with the [[woodblock print]] books of the Edo period.  This suggests that ''otogi-zōshi'' were meant to be read aloud, with the illustrations serving as reference guides.

Because the stories were frequently recited before an audience, many narratives are simple, with little description, depth, or development. Others, however, are more poetically allusive, suggesting composition by literati. Many of the tales are straightforward [[quest]] narratives, often revolving around a single heroic figure.  In order to advance the plot, the passage of time is swift, with characters sometimes aging years or decades in the space of a single sentence.

The vast topical range of ''otogi-zōshi'' discourages easy generalization.  The subject matter includes worldly concerns (love, marriage, family); spiritual matters (the pursuit of enlightenment, encounters with manifestations of the Buddha); martial adventures; farce, and supernatural fantasy.  While some of the stories exhibit a clear didactic agenda, most ''otogi-zōshi'' appear to have been composed primarily for the sake of entertainment.
--><!-- https://ja.Wikipedia.org/wiki/市古貞次 -->

{{Short description|Japanese literary genre}}
{{about|medieval Japanese illustrated short stories|the anime series|Otogi Zoshi (anime)|the short stories by Osamu Dazai|Otogi-zōshi (Dazai)}}
{{one source|date=January 2023}}
{{italic title}}
[[Image:Tale of Issun Bōshi from Otogi-zōshi.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3|right|Illustration from {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} tale, published {{c.|1725}}]]

{{nihongo||御伽草子|'''Otogi-zōshi'''}} are a group of about 350 Japanese prose narratives written primarily in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (1392–1573).  These illustrated short stories, which remain unattributed, together form one of the representative literary genres of the Japanese medieval era.

== Overview ==
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} is a general term for narrative literature written between the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (approximately 1336–1573) and the beginning of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603–1867).{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} The term originates with a mid-Edo collection of 23 stories, titled {{nihongo||御伽文庫|Otogi Bunko}} or {{nihongo||御伽草紙/御伽草子|Otogi-zōshi}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} It later came to denote other works of the same genre and period.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Modern scholarship sometimes distinguishes between "true" {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}, covering only the 23 works included in the aforementioned collection, and other works that it instead terms {{nihongo||室町時代物語|Muromachi-jidai monogatari}} or {{nihongo||中世小説|chūsei shōsetsu}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} 

===List===
The 23 tales covered by the narrow definition are:
*{{nihongo||文正草子|[[Bunshō-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||鉢かづき|[[Hachikazuki]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||小町草紙|[[:en:Komachi Sōshi|Komachi Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||御曹子島渡り|[[Onzōshi Shima-watari]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||唐糸草紙|[[Karaito-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||木幡狐|[[Kowata-gitsune]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||七草草紙|[[Nanakusa Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||猿源氏草紙|[[Saru Genji Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||物くさ太郎|[[Monokusa Tarō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||さざれ石|[[Sazare-ishi (otogi-zōshi)|Sazare-ishi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||蛤の草紙|[[Hamaguri no Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||小敦盛|[[Ko-Atsumori]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||二十四孝|[[:en:Nijūshi-kō|Nijūshi-kō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||梵天国|[[Bongengoku]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||のせ猿草子|[[Nose-zaru Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||猫の草紙|[[Neko no Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||浜出|[[Hamade (otogi-zōshi)|Hamade]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||和泉式部|[[Izumi Shikibu (otogi-zōshi)|Izumi Shikibu]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||一寸法師|[[Issun-bōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||さいき|[[Saiki (otogi-zōshi)|Saiki]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||浦嶋太郎|[[:en:Urashima Tarō (otogi-zōshi)|Urashima Tarō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||横笛草紙|[[Yokobue-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||酒呑童子|[[:en:Shuten-dōji|Shutendōji]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

Under the broad definition, there are around 500 surviving examples of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Most are around 30–40 pages in length,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and are of uncertain date.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Their authors are also largely unknown,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} but whereas [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] and [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura]] {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Monogatari|Monogatari]]}} were almost all composed by members of the aristocracy, these works were composed by not just aristocrats but also [[:en:Buddhist monk|Buddhist monk]]s, hermits, educated members of the [[:en:Samurai|warrior class]].{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Some of the later {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} may have been written by members of the emerging urban merchant class.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Similarly, the works' intended readership was probably broader than the {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}} of earlier eras.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} They therefore have a wide variety of contents and draw material from various literary works of the past.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Based on their contents, scholars have divided them into six genres:{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||公家物|kuge-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||宗教物|shūkyō-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||武家物|buke-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||庶民物|shomin-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||外国物|gaikoku-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||異類物|irui-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

==={{transliteration|ja|Kuge-mono}}===
{{transliteration|ja|Kuge-mono}} are tales of the aristocracy.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} They mark a continuation of the earlier {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}} literature,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and are noted for the influence of ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Many of them are rewritten or abridged versions of earlier works.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Among the romantic works in this sub-genre are {{nihongo||忍音物語|[[Shinobine Monogatari]]}} and {{nihongo||若草物語|[[Wakakusa Monogatari]]}},{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and most end sadly with the characters cutting themselves off from society ({{nihongo||悲恋遁世|hiren tonsei}}).{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

==Categories==
[[Image:Otogizoshi4.jpg|thumb|upright=1.5|left|Cover from volume of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} tales, published {{c.|1725}}]]
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} have been broken down into multiple categories: tales of the [[:en:Aristocracy|Aristocracy]], which are derived from earlier works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''; religious tales; tales of warriors, often based on ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', the {{transliteration|ja|[[Taiheiki]]}}, ''[[:en:The Tale of the Soga|The Tale of the Soga]]'' and the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Gikeiki|Gikeiki]]}} (''The Tale of Yoshitsune''); tales of foreign countries, based on the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatarishū]]}}. The most well-known of the tales, however, are retellings of familiar [[Japanese mythology|legend]]s and [[Japanese folklore|folktale]]s, such as {{transliteration|ja|[[Issun-bōshi]]}}, the story of a one-inch-tall boy who overcomes countless obstacles to achieve success in the capital.

== Origins of the term {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} ==
The term {{transliteration|ja|otogi}} literally means '', with the full name of the genre translating to 'companion tale'. This designation, however, did not come into use until 1725, when a publisher in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]] released a set of 23 illustrated booklets titled {{transliteration|ja|[[Shūgen otogibunko]]}} (''Fortuitous Companion Library''). As other publishers produced their own versions of {{transliteration|ja|Shūgen otogibunko}}, they began referring to the set of tales as {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}. Gradually the term came to describe any work from the Muromachi or early Edo period that exhibited the same general style as the tales in {{transliteration|ja|Shūgen otogibunko}}.

== History of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} scholarship ==
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} came to the attention of modern [[:en:Literary criticism|literary historian]]s in the late 19th century. For the most part, scholars have been critical of this genre, dismissing it for its perceived faults when compared to the aristocratic literature of the Heian and Kamakura periods. As a result, standardized [[:en:Education in Japan|Japanese school]] [[:en:Textbook|Textbook]]s often omit any reference to {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} from their discussions of [[:en:medieval Japanese literature|medieval Japanese literature]]. Recent studies, however, have contradicted this critical stance, highlighting the vitality and inherent appeal of this underappreciated genre. The term {{transliteration|ja|chusei shosetsu}} ('medieval novels'), coined by eminent scholar Ichiko Teiji, attempts to situate the tales within a narrative continuum.

== List of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} ==
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Aisome-gawa (otogi-zōshi)|Aisome-gawa]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Aki no Yo no Naga Monogatari|Aki no Yo no Naga Monogatari]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ame no Wakahiko sōshi (otogi-zōshi)|Ame no Wakahiko sōshi]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Komachi Sōshi|Komachi Sōshi]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Koshikibu (one-volume otogi-zōshi)|Koshikibu]]}} (I)
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Koshikibu (two-volume otogi-zōshi)|Koshikibu]]}} (II)
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Urashima Tarō (otogi-zōshi)|Urashima Tarō (otogi-zōshi)]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Utatane no Sōshi|Utatane no Sōshi]]}}

ORIGINAL, UNSOURCED DESCRIPTION (may be accurate, and wording may be better paraphrase of sourced version, so leaving intact but commented out for now):

This type of short prose narrative from [[Japan]] is often considered a transitional genre, which bridges the gap between the courtly literature of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794-1160) and the more [[Plebs|plebeian]] ''[[kanazōshi]]'' (tales written in [[kana]]) and ''[[ukiyo-zōshi]]'' (tales of the floating world) of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1600–1868).  The stories were originally recorded in both illustrated scrolls or booklets ([[Nara-ehon]]) which link the picture scrolls of the Heian era with the [[woodblock print]] books of the Edo period.  This suggests that ''otogi-zōshi'' were meant to be read aloud, with the illustrations serving as reference guides.

Because the stories were frequently recited before an audience, many narratives are simple, with little description, depth, or development. Others, however, are more poetically allusive, suggesting composition by literati. Many of the tales are straightforward [[quest]] narratives, often revolving around a single heroic figure.  In order to advance the plot, the passage of time is swift, with characters sometimes aging years or decades in the space of a single sentence.

The vast topical range of ''otogi-zōshi'' discourages easy generalization.  The subject matter includes worldly concerns (love, marriage, family); spiritual matters (the pursuit of enlightenment, encounters with manifestations of the Buddha); martial adventures; farce, and supernatural fantasy.  While some of the stories exhibit a clear didactic agenda, most ''otogi-zōshi'' appear to have been composed primarily for the sake of entertainment.
--><!-- https://ja.Wikipedia.org/wiki/市古貞次 -->

Wikidata name: otogizōshi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Japanese literary genre}}
{{about|medieval Japanese illustrated short stories|the anime series|Otogi Zoshi (anime)|the short stories by Osamu Dazai|Otogi-zōshi (Dazai)}}
{{one source|date=January 2023}}
{{italic title}}
[[Image:Tale of Issun Bōshi from Otogi-zōshi.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3|right|Illustration from {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} tale, published {{c.|1725}}]]

{{nihongo||御伽草子|'''Otogi-zōshi'''}} are a group of about 350 Japanese prose narratives written primarily in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (1392–1573).  These illustrated short stories, which remain unattributed, together form one of the representative literary genres of the Japanese medieval era.

== Overview ==
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} is a general term for narrative literature written between the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (approximately 1336–1573) and the beginning of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603–1867).{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} The term originates with a mid-Edo collection of 23 stories, titled {{nihongo||御伽文庫|Otogi Bunko}} or {{nihongo||御伽草紙/御伽草子|Otogi-zōshi}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} It later came to denote other works of the same genre and period.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Modern scholarship sometimes distinguishes between "true" {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}, covering only the 23 works included in the aforementioned collection, and other works that it instead terms {{nihongo||室町時代物語|Muromachi-jidai monogatari}} or {{nihongo||中世小説|chūsei shōsetsu}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} 

===List===
The 23 tales covered by the narrow definition are:
*{{nihongo||文正草子|[[Bunshō-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||鉢かづき|[[Hachikazuki]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||小町草紙|[[:en:Komachi Sōshi|Komachi Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||御曹子島渡り|[[Onzōshi Shima-watari]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||唐糸草紙|[[Karaito-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||木幡狐|[[Kowata-gitsune]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||七草草紙|[[Nanakusa Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||猿源氏草紙|[[Saru Genji Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||物くさ太郎|[[Monokusa Tarō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||さざれ石|[[Sazare-ishi (otogi-zōshi)|Sazare-ishi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||蛤の草紙|[[Hamaguri no Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||小敦盛|[[Ko-Atsumori]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||二十四孝|[[:en:Nijūshi-kō|Nijūshi-kō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||梵天国|[[Bongengoku]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||のせ猿草子|[[Nose-zaru Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||猫の草紙|[[Neko no Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||浜出|[[Hamade (otogi-zōshi)|Hamade]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||和泉式部|[[Izumi Shikibu (otogi-zōshi)|Izumi Shikibu]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||一寸法師|[[Issun-bōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||さいき|[[Saiki (otogi-zōshi)|Saiki]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||浦嶋太郎|[[:en:Urashima Tarō (otogi-zōshi)|Urashima Tarō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||横笛草紙|[[Yokobue-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||酒呑童子|[[:en:Shuten-dōji|Shutendōji]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

Under the broad definition, there are around 500 surviving examples of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Most are around 30–40 pages in length,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and are of uncertain date.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Their authors are also largely unknown,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} but whereas [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] and [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura]] {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Monogatari|Monogatari]]}} were almost all composed by members of the aristocracy, these works were composed by not just aristocrats but also [[:en:Buddhist monk|Buddhist monk]]s, hermits, educated members of the [[:en:Samurai|warrior class]].{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Some of the later {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} may have been written by members of the emerging urban merchant class.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Similarly, the works' intended readership was probably broader than the {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}} of earlier eras.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} They therefore have a wide variety of contents and draw material from various literary works of the past.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Based on their contents, scholars have divided them into six genres:{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||公家物|kuge-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||宗教物|shūkyō-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||武家物|buke-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||庶民物|shomin-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||外国物|gaikoku-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||異類物|irui-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

==={{transliteration|ja|Kuge-mono}}===
{{transliteration|ja|Kuge-mono}} are tales of the aristocracy.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} They mark a continuation of the earlier {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}} literature,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and are noted for the influence of ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Many of them are rewritten or abridged versions of earlier works.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Among the romantic works in this sub-genre are {{nihongo||忍音物語|[[Shinobine Monogatari]]}} and {{nihongo||若草物語|[[Wakakusa Monogatari]]}},{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and most end sadly with the characters cutting themselves off from society ({{nihongo||悲恋遁世|hiren tonsei}}).{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

==Categories==
[[Image:Otogizoshi4.jpg|thumb|upright=1.5|left|Cover from volume of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} tales, published {{c.|1725}}]]
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} have been broken down into multiple categories: tales of the [[:en:Aristocracy|Aristocracy]], which are derived from earlier works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''; religious tales; tales of warriors, often based on ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', the {{transliteration|ja|[[Taiheiki]]}}, ''[[:en:The Tale of the Soga|The Tale of the Soga]]'' and the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Gikeiki|Gikeiki]]}} (''The Tale of Yoshitsune''); tales of foreign countries, based on the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatarishū]]}}. The most well-known of the tales, however, are retellings of familiar [[Japanese mythology|legend]]s and [[Japanese folklore|folktale]]s, such as {{transliteration|ja|[[Issun-bōshi]]}}, the story of a one-inch-tall boy who overcomes countless obstacles to achieve success in the capital.

As a result, standardized [[:en:Education in Japan|Japanese school]] [[:en:Textbook|Textbook]]s often omit any reference to {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} from their discussions of [[:en:medieval Japanese literature|medieval Japanese literature]]. [[en:Otogi-zōshi]]

<!-- "都市人" almost certainly means this, although a more specific source would still be preferable. --><!-- Not technically verified in the source, but a literal translation of the word the source uses. --><!-- entering Buddhist orders? --><!--

ORIGINAL, UNSOURCED DESCRIPTION (may be accurate, and wording may be better paraphrase of sourced version, so leaving intact but commented out for now):

This type of short prose narrative from [[Japan]] is often considered a transitional genre, which bridges the gap between the courtly literature of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794-1160) and the more [[Plebs|plebeian]] ''[[kanazōshi]]'' (tales written in [[kana]]) and ''[[ukiyo-zōshi]]'' (tales of the floating world) of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1600–1868).  The stories were originally recorded in both illustrated scrolls or booklets ([[Nara-ehon]]) which link the picture scrolls of the Heian era with the [[woodblock print]] books of the Edo period.  This suggests that ''otogi-zōshi'' were meant to be read aloud, with the illustrations serving as reference guides.

Because the stories were frequently recited before an audience, many narratives are simple, with little description, depth, or development. Others, however, are more poetically allusive, suggesting composition by literati. Many of the tales are straightforward [[quest]] narratives, often revolving around a single heroic figure.  In order to advance the plot, the passage of time is swift, with characters sometimes aging years or decades in the space of a single sentence.

The vast topical range of ''otogi-zōshi'' discourages easy generalization.  The subject matter includes worldly concerns (love, marriage, family); spiritual matters (the pursuit of enlightenment, encounters with manifestations of the Buddha); martial adventures; farce, and supernatural fantasy.  While some of the stories exhibit a clear didactic agenda, most ''otogi-zōshi'' appear to have been composed primarily for the sake of entertainment.
--><!-- https://ja.Wikipedia.org/wiki/市古貞次 -->

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q82440]]
Wikidata name: otogizōshi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Japanese literary genre}}
{{about|medieval Japanese illustrated short stories|the anime series|Otogi Zoshi (anime)|the short stories by Osamu Dazai|Otogi-zōshi (Dazai)}}
{{one source|date=January 2023}}
{{italic title}}
[[Image:Tale of Issun Bōshi from Otogi-zōshi.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3|right|Illustration from {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} tale, published {{c.|1725}}]]

{{nihongo||御伽草子|'''Otogi-zōshi'''}} are a group of about 350 Japanese prose narratives written primarily in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (1392–1573).  These illustrated short stories, which remain unattributed, together form one of the representative literary genres of the Japanese medieval era.

== Overview ==
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} is a general term for narrative literature written between the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (approximately 1336–1573) and the beginning of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603–1867).{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} The term originates with a mid-Edo collection of 23 stories, titled {{nihongo||御伽文庫|Otogi Bunko}} or {{nihongo||御伽草紙/御伽草子|Otogi-zōshi}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} It later came to denote other works of the same genre and period.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Modern scholarship sometimes distinguishes between "true" {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}, covering only the 23 works included in the aforementioned collection, and other works that it instead terms {{nihongo||室町時代物語|Muromachi-jidai monogatari}} or {{nihongo||中世小説|chūsei shōsetsu}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} 

===List===
The 23 tales covered by the narrow definition are:
*{{nihongo||文正草子|[[Bunshō-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||鉢かづき|[[Hachikazuki]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||小町草紙|[[:en:Komachi Sōshi|Komachi Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||御曹子島渡り|[[Onzōshi Shima-watari]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||唐糸草紙|[[Karaito-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||木幡狐|[[Kowata-gitsune]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||七草草紙|[[Nanakusa Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||猿源氏草紙|[[Saru Genji Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||物くさ太郎|[[Monokusa Tarō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||さざれ石|[[Sazare-ishi (otogi-zōshi)|Sazare-ishi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||蛤の草紙|[[Hamaguri no Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||小敦盛|[[Ko-Atsumori]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||二十四孝|[[:en:Nijūshi-kō|Nijūshi-kō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||梵天国|[[Bongengoku]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||のせ猿草子|[[Nose-zaru Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||猫の草紙|[[Neko no Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||浜出|[[Hamade (otogi-zōshi)|Hamade]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||和泉式部|[[Izumi Shikibu (otogi-zōshi)|Izumi Shikibu]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||一寸法師|[[Issun-bōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||さいき|[[Saiki (otogi-zōshi)|Saiki]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||浦嶋太郎|[[:en:Urashima Tarō (otogi-zōshi)|Urashima Tarō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||横笛草紙|[[Yokobue-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||酒呑童子|[[:en:Shuten-dōji|Shutendōji]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

Under the broad definition, there are around 500 surviving examples of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Most are around 30–40 pages in length,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and are of uncertain date.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Their authors are also largely unknown,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} but whereas [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] and [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura]] {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Monogatari|Monogatari]]}} were almost all composed by members of the aristocracy, these works were composed by not just aristocrats but also [[:en:Buddhist monk|Buddhist monk]]s, hermits, educated members of the [[:en:Samurai|warrior class]].{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Some of the later {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} may have been written by members of the emerging urban merchant class.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Similarly, the works' intended readership was probably broader than the {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}} of earlier eras.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} They therefore have a wide variety of contents and draw material from various literary works of the past.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Based on their contents, scholars have divided them into six genres:{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||公家物|kuge-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||宗教物|shūkyō-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||武家物|buke-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||庶民物|shomin-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||外国物|gaikoku-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||異類物|irui-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

==={{transliteration|ja|Kuge-mono}}===
{{transliteration|ja|Kuge-mono}} are tales of the aristocracy.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} They mark a continuation of the earlier {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}} literature,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and are noted for the influence of ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Many of them are rewritten or abridged versions of earlier works.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Among the romantic works in this sub-genre are {{nihongo||忍音物語|[[Shinobine Monogatari]]}} and {{nihongo||若草物語|[[Wakakusa Monogatari]]}},{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and most end sadly with the characters cutting themselves off from society ({{nihongo||悲恋遁世|hiren tonsei}}).{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

==Categories==
[[Image:Otogizoshi4.jpg|thumb|upright=1.5|left|Cover from volume of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} tales, published {{c.|1725}}]]
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} have been broken down into multiple categories: tales of the [[:en:Aristocracy|Aristocracy]], which are derived from earlier works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''; religious tales; tales of warriors, often based on ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', the {{transliteration|ja|[[Taiheiki]]}}, ''[[:en:The Tale of the Soga|The Tale of the Soga]]'' and the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Gikeiki|Gikeiki]]}} (''The Tale of Yoshitsune''); tales of foreign countries, based on the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatarishū]]}}. The most well-known of the tales, however, are retellings of familiar [[Japanese mythology|legend]]s and [[Japanese folklore|folktale]]s, such as {{transliteration|ja|[[Issun-bōshi]]}}, the story of a one-inch-tall boy who overcomes countless obstacles to achieve success in the capital.

== Origins of the term {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} ==
The term {{transliteration|ja|otogi}} literally means '', with the full name of the genre translating to 'companion tale'. This designation, however, did not come into use until 1725, when a publisher in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]] released a set of 23 illustrated booklets titled {{transliteration|ja|[[Shūgen otogibunko]]}} (''Fortuitous Companion Library''). As other publishers produced their own versions of {{transliteration|ja|Shūgen otogibunko}}, they began referring to the set of tales as {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}. Gradually the term came to describe any work from the Muromachi or early Edo period that exhibited the same general style as the tales in {{transliteration|ja|Shūgen otogibunko}}.

== History of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} scholarship ==
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} came to the attention of modern [[:en:Literary criticism|literary historian]]s in the late 19th century. For the most part, scholars have been critical of this genre, dismissing it for its perceived faults when compared to the aristocratic literature of the Heian and Kamakura periods. As a result, standardized [[:en:Education in Japan|Japanese school]] [[:en:Textbook|Textbook]]s often omit any reference to {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} from their discussions of [[:en:medieval Japanese literature|medieval Japanese literature]]. Recent studies, however, have contradicted this critical stance, highlighting the vitality and inherent appeal of this underappreciated genre. The term {{transliteration|ja|chusei shosetsu}} ('medieval novels'), coined by eminent scholar Ichiko Teiji, attempts to situate the tales within a narrative continuum.

== List of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} ==
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Aisome-gawa (otogi-zōshi)|Aisome-gawa]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Aki no Yo no Naga Monogatari|Aki no Yo no Naga Monogatari]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ame no Wakahiko sōshi (otogi-zōshi)|Ame no Wakahiko sōshi]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Komachi Sōshi|Komachi Sōshi]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Koshikibu (one-volume otogi-zōshi)|Koshikibu]]}} (I)
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Koshikibu (two-volume otogi-zōshi)|Koshikibu]]}} (II)
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Urashima Tarō (otogi-zōshi)|Urashima Tarō (otogi-zōshi)]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Utatane no Sōshi|Utatane no Sōshi]]}}

Wikidata name: otogizōshi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Japanese literary genre}}
{{about|medieval Japanese illustrated short stories|the anime series|Otogi Zoshi (anime)|the short stories by Osamu Dazai|Otogi-zōshi (Dazai)}}
{{one source|date=January 2023}}
{{italic title}}
[[Image:Tale of Issun Bōshi from Otogi-zōshi.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3|right|Illustration from {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} tale, published {{c.|1725}}]]

{{nihongo||御伽草子|'''Otogi-zōshi'''}} are a group of about 350 Japanese prose narratives written primarily in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (1392–1573).  These illustrated short stories, which remain unattributed, together form one of the representative literary genres of the Japanese medieval era.

== Overview ==
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} is a general term for narrative literature written between the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] (approximately 1336–1573) and the beginning of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603–1867).{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} The term originates with a mid-Edo collection of 23 stories, titled {{nihongo||御伽文庫|Otogi Bunko}} or {{nihongo||御伽草紙/御伽草子|Otogi-zōshi}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} It later came to denote other works of the same genre and period.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Modern scholarship sometimes distinguishes between "true" {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}, covering only the 23 works included in the aforementioned collection, and other works that it instead terms {{nihongo||室町時代物語|Muromachi-jidai monogatari}} or {{nihongo||中世小説|chūsei shōsetsu}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} 

===List===
The 23 tales covered by the narrow definition are:
*{{nihongo||文正草子|[[Bunshō-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||鉢かづき|[[Hachikazuki]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||小町草紙|[[:en:Komachi Sōshi|Komachi Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||御曹子島渡り|[[Onzōshi Shima-watari]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||唐糸草紙|[[Karaito-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||木幡狐|[[Kowata-gitsune]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||七草草紙|[[Nanakusa Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||猿源氏草紙|[[Saru Genji Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||物くさ太郎|[[Monokusa Tarō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||さざれ石|[[Sazare-ishi (otogi-zōshi)|Sazare-ishi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||蛤の草紙|[[Hamaguri no Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||小敦盛|[[Ko-Atsumori]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||二十四孝|[[:en:Nijūshi-kō|Nijūshi-kō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||梵天国|[[Bongengoku]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||のせ猿草子|[[Nose-zaru Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||猫の草紙|[[Neko no Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||浜出|[[Hamade (otogi-zōshi)|Hamade]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||和泉式部|[[Izumi Shikibu (otogi-zōshi)|Izumi Shikibu]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||一寸法師|[[Issun-bōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||さいき|[[Saiki (otogi-zōshi)|Saiki]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||浦嶋太郎|[[:en:Urashima Tarō (otogi-zōshi)|Urashima Tarō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||横笛草紙|[[Yokobue-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||酒呑童子|[[:en:Shuten-dōji|Shutendōji]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

Under the broad definition, there are around 500 surviving examples of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Most are around 30–40 pages in length,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and are of uncertain date.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Their authors are also largely unknown,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} but whereas [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] and [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura]] {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Monogatari|Monogatari]]}} were almost all composed by members of the aristocracy, these works were composed by not just aristocrats but also [[:en:Buddhist monk|Buddhist monk]]s, hermits, educated members of the [[:en:Samurai|warrior class]].{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Some of the later {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} may have been written by members of the emerging urban merchant class.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Similarly, the works' intended readership was probably broader than the {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}} of earlier eras.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} They therefore have a wide variety of contents and draw material from various literary works of the past.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Based on their contents, scholars have divided them into six genres:{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||公家物|kuge-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||宗教物|shūkyō-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||武家物|buke-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||庶民物|shomin-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||外国物|gaikoku-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||異類物|irui-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

==={{transliteration|ja|Kuge-mono}}===
{{transliteration|ja|Kuge-mono}} are tales of the aristocracy.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} They mark a continuation of the earlier {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}} literature,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and are noted for the influence of ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Many of them are rewritten or abridged versions of earlier works.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Among the romantic works in this sub-genre are {{nihongo||忍音物語|[[Shinobine Monogatari]]}} and {{nihongo||若草物語|[[Wakakusa Monogatari]]}},{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and most end sadly with the characters cutting themselves off from society ({{nihongo||悲恋遁世|hiren tonsei}}).{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

==Categories==
[[Image:Otogizoshi4.jpg|thumb|upright=1.5|left|Cover from volume of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} tales, published {{c.|1725}}]]
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} have been broken down into multiple categories: tales of the [[:en:Aristocracy|Aristocracy]], which are derived from earlier works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''; religious tales; tales of warriors, often based on ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', the {{transliteration|ja|[[Taiheiki]]}}, ''[[:en:The Tale of the Soga|The Tale of the Soga]]'' and the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Gikeiki|Gikeiki]]}} (''The Tale of Yoshitsune''); tales of foreign countries, based on the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatarishū]]}}. The most well-known of the tales, however, are retellings of familiar [[Japanese mythology|legend]]s and [[Japanese folklore|folktale]]s, such as {{transliteration|ja|[[Issun-bōshi]]}}, the story of a one-inch-tall boy who overcomes countless obstacles to achieve success in the capital.

== Origins of the term {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} ==
The term {{transliteration|ja|otogi}} literally means '', with the full name of the genre translating to 'companion tale'. This designation, however, did not come into use until 1725, when a publisher in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]] released a set of 23 illustrated booklets titled {{transliteration|ja|[[Shūgen otogibunko]]}} (''Fortuitous Companion Library''). As other publishers produced their own versions of {{transliteration|ja|Shūgen otogibunko}}, they began referring to the set of tales as {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}. Gradually the term came to describe any work from the Muromachi or early Edo period that exhibited the same general style as the tales in {{transliteration|ja|Shūgen otogibunko}}.

== History of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} scholarship ==
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} came to the attention of modern [[:en:Literary criticism|literary historian]]s in the late 19th century. For the most part, scholars have been critical of this genre, dismissing it for its perceived faults when compared to the aristocratic literature of the Heian and Kamakura periods. As a result, standardized [[:en:Education in Japan|Japanese school]] [[:en:Textbook|Textbook]]s often omit any reference to {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} from their discussions of [[:en:medieval Japanese literature|medieval Japanese literature]]. Recent studies, however, have contradicted this critical stance, highlighting the vitality and inherent appeal of this underappreciated genre. The term {{transliteration|ja|chusei shosetsu}} ('medieval novels'), coined by eminent scholar Ichiko Teiji, attempts to situate the tales within a narrative continuum.

== List of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} ==
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Aisome-gawa (otogi-zōshi)|Aisome-gawa]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Aki no Yo no Naga Monogatari|Aki no Yo no Naga Monogatari]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ame no Wakahiko sōshi (otogi-zōshi)|Ame no Wakahiko sōshi]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Komachi Sōshi|Komachi Sōshi]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Koshikibu (one-volume otogi-zōshi)|Koshikibu]]}} (I)
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Koshikibu (two-volume otogi-zōshi)|Koshikibu]]}} (II)
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Urashima Tarō (otogi-zōshi)|Urashima Tarō (otogi-zōshi)]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Utatane no Sōshi|Utatane no Sōshi]]}}

<!-- "都市人" almost certainly means this, although a more specific source would still be preferable. --><!-- Not technically verified in the source, but a literal translation of the word the source uses. --><!-- entering Buddhist orders? --><!--

ORIGINAL, UNSOURCED DESCRIPTION (may be accurate, and wording may be better paraphrase of sourced version, so leaving intact but commented out for now):

This type of short prose narrative from [[Japan]] is often considered a transitional genre, which bridges the gap between the courtly literature of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794-1160) and the more [[Plebs|plebeian]] ''[[kanazōshi]]'' (tales written in [[kana]]) and ''[[ukiyo-zōshi]]'' (tales of the floating world) of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1600–1868).  The stories were originally recorded in both illustrated scrolls or booklets ([[Nara-ehon]]) which link the picture scrolls of the Heian era with the [[woodblock print]] books of the Edo period.  This suggests that ''otogi-zōshi'' were meant to be read aloud, with the illustrations serving as reference guides.

Because the stories were frequently recited before an audience, many narratives are simple, with little description, depth, or development. Others, however, are more poetically allusive, suggesting composition by literati. Many of the tales are straightforward [[quest]] narratives, often revolving around a single heroic figure.  In order to advance the plot, the passage of time is swift, with characters sometimes aging years or decades in the space of a single sentence.

The vast topical range of ''otogi-zōshi'' discourages easy generalization.  The subject matter includes worldly concerns (love, marriage, family); spiritual matters (the pursuit of enlightenment, encounters with manifestations of the Buddha); martial adventures; farce, and supernatural fantasy.  While some of the stories exhibit a clear didactic agenda, most ''otogi-zōshi'' appear to have been composed primarily for the sake of entertainment.
--><!-- https://ja.Wikipedia.org/wiki/市古貞次 -->

The 23 tales covered by the narrow definition are:
*{{nihongo||文正草子|[[Bunshō-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||鉢かづき|[[Hachikazuki]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

*{{nihongo||木幡狐|[[Kowata-gitsune]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||七草草紙|[[Nanakusa Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||猿源氏草紙|[[Saru Genji Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||物くさ太郎|[[Monokusa Tarō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||さざれ石|[[Sazare-ishi (otogi-zōshi)|Sazare-ishi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||蛤の草紙|[[Hamaguri no Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||小敦盛|[[Ko-Atsumori]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||二十四孝|[[:en:Nijūshi-kō|Nijūshi-kō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||梵天国|[[Bongengoku]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||のせ猿草子|[[Nose-zaru Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||猫の草紙|[[Neko no Sōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||浜出|[[Hamade (otogi-zōshi)|Hamade]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||和泉式部|[[Izumi Shikibu (otogi-zōshi)|Izumi Shikibu]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||一寸法師|[[Issun-bōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||さいき|[[Saiki (otogi-zōshi)|Saiki]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||浦嶋太郎|[[:en:Urashima Tarō (otogi-zōshi)|Urashima Tarō]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||横笛草紙|[[Yokobue-zōshi]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
*{{nihongo||酒呑童子|[[:en:Shuten-dōji|Shutendōji]]}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

Under the broad definition, there are around 500 surviving examples of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Most are around 30–40 pages in length,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and are of uncertain date.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Their authors are also largely unknown,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} but whereas [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] and [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura]] {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Monogatari|Monogatari]]}} were almost all composed by members of the aristocracy, these works were composed by not just aristocrats but also [[:en:Buddhist monk|Buddhist monk]]s, hermits, educated members of the [[:en:Samurai|warrior class]].{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Some of the later {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} may have been written by members of the emerging urban merchant class.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Similarly, the works' intended readership was probably broader than the {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}} of earlier eras.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} They therefore have a wide variety of contents and draw material from various literary works of the past.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Based on their contents, scholars have divided them into six genres:{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||公家物|kuge-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||宗教物|shūkyō-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||武家物|buke-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||庶民物|shomin-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||外国物|gaikoku-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}
#{{nihongo||異類物|irui-mono}}{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

==={{transliteration|ja|Kuge-mono}}===
{{transliteration|ja|Kuge-mono}} are tales of the aristocracy.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} They mark a continuation of the earlier {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}} literature,{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and are noted for the influence of ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Many of them are rewritten or abridged versions of earlier works.{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} Among the romantic works in this sub-genre are {{nihongo||忍音物語|[[Shinobine Monogatari]]}} and {{nihongo||若草物語|[[Wakakusa Monogatari]]}},{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}} and most end sadly with the characters cutting themselves off from society ({{nihongo||悲恋遁世|hiren tonsei}}).{{sfnm|1a1=Ichiko|1y=1983|1p=498}}

==Categories==
[[Image:Otogizoshi4.jpg|thumb|upright=1.5|left|Cover from volume of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} tales, published {{c.|1725}}]]
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} have been broken down into multiple categories: tales of the [[:en:Aristocracy|Aristocracy]], which are derived from earlier works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''; religious tales; tales of warriors, often based on ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', the {{transliteration|ja|[[Taiheiki]]}}, ''[[:en:The Tale of the Soga|The Tale of the Soga]]'' and the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Gikeiki|Gikeiki]]}} (''The Tale of Yoshitsune''); tales of foreign countries, based on the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatarishū]]}}. The most well-known of the tales, however, are retellings of familiar [[Japanese mythology|legend]]s and [[Japanese folklore|folktale]]s, such as {{transliteration|ja|[[Issun-bōshi]]}}, the story of a one-inch-tall boy who overcomes countless obstacles to achieve success in the capital.

== Origins of the term {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} ==
The term {{transliteration|ja|otogi}} literally means '', with the full name of the genre translating to 'companion tale'. This designation, however, did not come into use until 1725, when a publisher in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]] released a set of 23 illustrated booklets titled {{transliteration|ja|[[Shūgen otogibunko]]}} (''Fortuitous Companion Library''). As other publishers produced their own versions of {{transliteration|ja|Shūgen otogibunko}}, they began referring to the set of tales as {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}}. Gradually the term came to describe any work from the Muromachi or early Edo period that exhibited the same general style as the tales in {{transliteration|ja|Shūgen otogibunko}}.

== History of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} scholarship ==
{{transliteration|ja|Otogi-zōshi}} came to the attention of modern [[:en:Literary criticism|literary historian]]s in the late 19th century. For the most part, scholars have been critical of this genre, dismissing it for its perceived faults when compared to the aristocratic literature of the Heian and Kamakura periods. As a result, standardized [[:en:Education in Japan|Japanese school]] [[:en:Textbook|Textbook]]s often omit any reference to {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} from their discussions of [[:en:medieval Japanese literature|medieval Japanese literature]]. Recent studies, however, have contradicted this critical stance, highlighting the vitality and inherent appeal of this underappreciated genre. The term {{transliteration|ja|chusei shosetsu}} ('medieval novels'), coined by eminent scholar Ichiko Teiji, attempts to situate the tales within a narrative continuum.

== List of {{transliteration|ja|otogi-zōshi}} ==
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Aisome-gawa (otogi-zōshi)|Aisome-gawa]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Aki no Yo no Naga Monogatari|Aki no Yo no Naga Monogatari]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ame no Wakahiko sōshi (otogi-zōshi)|Ame no Wakahiko sōshi]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Komachi Sōshi|Komachi Sōshi]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Koshikibu (one-volume otogi-zōshi)|Koshikibu]]}} (I)
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Koshikibu (two-volume otogi-zōshi)|Koshikibu]]}} (II)
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Urashima Tarō (otogi-zōshi)|Urashima Tarō (otogi-zōshi)]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Utatane no Sōshi|Utatane no Sōshi]]}}
